summary:
The coronation of Napoleon Bonaparte as Emperor of the French on 2 December 1804 at Notre-Dame Cathedral in Paris was one of the most dramatic political spectacles of the modern era — the moment when a general of the French Republic, drawing on revolutionary legitimacy and military achievement, crowned himself head of a new imperial dynasty and announced that the age of kings ruling by divine right had given way to an era of merit and power.

The coronation was engineered to be both an acknowledgment of traditional European royal ceremony and a deliberate departure from it. Napoleon had Pope Pius VII travel from Rome to consecrate the ceremony, lending it papal legitimacy that reassured Catholic France and impressed European monarchs. But in the ceremony's climactic moment, Napoleon took the crown from the Pope's hands and placed it on his own head — demonstrating that his power derived not from God's representative but from his own genius and the will of the French people. He then crowned his wife Josephine Empress.

The coronation followed Napoleon's proclamation as Consul for Life in 1802 and his reestablishment of hereditary succession. The [[event:2600|Napoleonic Code]] had already been promulgated earlier in 1804, and the new empire adopted Roman imperial imagery throughout — the eagle, the laurel wreath, the title 'Majesty.' Napoleon sought to found a dynasty that would last centuries and create a new European order centered on France.

David's enormous painting of the coronation ceremony, completed in 1807, is one of the most famous works of the Napoleonic era. The empire Napoleon founded lasted only a decade before his defeat and exile, but his imperial model — the charismatic leader ruling in the name of national will rather than divine right — proved enormously influential, setting the template for the nationalist dictatorships and populist movements that would follow in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.
